metadata.subject: Watercolour painting of a photorealistic blue eye
metadata.expanded_subject: A watercolour painting of a dark blue eye, focusing on soft washes of blue and subtle shading to capture the depth and texture of the iris. The style should emulate the fluidity and translucency of traditional watercolour, with gentle transitions and a dreamy, ethereal quality.

painting_plan.subject: Watercolour painting of a photorealistic blue eye
painting_plan.expanded_subject: A watercolour painting of a dark blue eye, focusing on soft washes of blue and subtle shading to capture the depth and texture of the iris. The style should emulate the fluidity and translucency of traditional watercolour, with gentle transitions and a dreamy, ethereal quality.

layers.name: Luminous background wash
layers.description: A soft, wet-on-wet background wash covering the entire 800x600 canvas. Pale ivory and soft cream tones dominate, with faint hints of cool blue bleeding in at the edges to suggest the ambient colour of the eye's surroundings. The wash should be uneven and organic, mimicking the natural bloom of watercolour on wet paper.
layers.techniques: Broad, sweeping wet-on-wet washes across the entire canvas. Allow colours to bleed and merge naturally. Use wide, loose arcs to suggest the soft diffusion of light. Light splatter at edges for texture.
layers.shapes: Horizontal and diagonal bands of colour, soft cloud-like blooms, irregular organic edges where colours meet
layers.highlights: Keep the centre of the canvas lightest — near pure white or ivory — to reserve luminosity for the eye itself. Allow cool blue to drift in from the upper corners to set the overall cool, dreamy atmosphere.

layers.name: Skin and eyelid base tones
layers.description: Establish the surrounding skin of the eyelids, brow area, and under-eye region. The eye occupies the centre of the canvas, roughly 500x300 pixels in extent. Paint the upper eyelid curving from upper-left to upper-right, and the lower eyelid curving below. Use warm peachy-beige tones with subtle rose and shadow hints in the creases and corners.
layers.techniques: Wet-on-wet washes for smooth skin transitions. Layered arcs following the curve of the eyelids. Soft polylines to suggest the gentle folds of skin. Allow edges to remain soft and undefined.
layers.shapes: Broad curved arcs for upper and lower eyelids, gentle crescent shapes for the eye socket shadow, soft organic forms for the brow ridge
layers.highlights: Lightest skin tones at the brow bone and directly below the lower lash line. Deeper rose-brown shadows in the inner corner and the crease of the upper eyelid to give dimensional form.

layers.name: Sclera and eye white
layers.description: Paint the white of the eye — the sclera — visible on either side of the iris in the centre of the canvas. This is not pure white but a soft, slightly blue-grey or warm off-white with faint pink veining near the inner and outer corners. The sclera fills the almond-shaped eye opening, framed by the eyelids above and below.
layers.techniques: Very dilute, pale washes laid with gentle arcs following the curve of the eye opening. Thin lines for subtle pink veining near the corners. Leave the brightest areas as near-untouched paper.
layers.shapes: Almond or lens-shaped region, soft curved arcs, thin delicate lines for veins
layers.highlights: The sclera should appear brightest near the inner highlight zone. A faint cool-blue shadow cast by the upper eyelid across the top of the sclera adds realism and depth.

layers.name: Iris base and mid-tones
layers.description: Paint the iris as a large circle centred on the canvas, approximately 180-200 pixels in diameter. Use deep navy, prussian blue, and cerulean washes to establish the rich, dark blue colour of the iris. Radiating lines from the pupil outward suggest the fibrous texture of the iris. The outer edge of the iris has a dark limbal ring.
layers.techniques: Concentric wet-on-wet washes from the outer limbal ring inward, darkest at the edge and slightly lighter mid-iris. Fine radiating lines from the pupil outward to suggest iris fibres. Allow blues to bloom and merge for a natural, organic look.
layers.shapes: Large circle for the iris overall, radiating lines from centre outward, a dark ring at the outer edge, concentric tonal bands
layers.highlights: The upper portion of the iris should be slightly darker, shadowed by the upper eyelid. The lower portion can be fractionally lighter and more saturated. The limbal ring (outer dark edge) should be the deepest tone in this layer.

layers.name: Pupil, iris detail, and lashes
layers.description: Paint the pupil as a deep, near-black circle at the centre of the iris, approximately 70-80 pixels in diameter. Add fine radiating detail lines across the iris in varied blues and teals to enhance texture. Paint the upper and lower eyelashes as fine, curved lines arcing outward from the eyelid edges. Add the dark eyelid margin lines.
layers.techniques: Solid dark wash for the pupil. Fine, confident brushstrokes for individual lashes, curving outward and tapering to a point. Delicate radiating lines across the iris for fibre texture. Dark line work for the eyelid margins.
layers.shapes: Dark circle for pupil, fine tapering curved lines for lashes fanning outward, thin straight and curved lines for iris detail
layers.highlights: Upper lashes should be longer, denser, and more dramatically curved. Lower lashes shorter and more sparse. The pupil edge should be crisp and dark. Iris detail lines should vary in opacity — some bold, some barely visible — for natural variation.

layers.name: Highlights, reflections, and final glazes
layers.description: Add the final luminous highlights and reflective details that bring the eye to life. A bright white or near-white circular highlight sits in the upper-left quadrant of the iris, partially overlapping the pupil. A secondary softer highlight appears in the lower-right of the iris. Add a final cool blue glaze over the iris to unify the tones. Subtle splatter suggests the sparkle and moisture of the eye surface.
layers.techniques: Reserve or apply opaque white for the primary catchlight highlight. Soft pale arcs for secondary reflections. Very fine splatter of pale blue-white for a sense of luminosity and moisture. A final dilute blue glaze arc over the upper iris to deepen shadow.
layers.shapes: Small bright circle or oval for the main catchlight, soft crescent for secondary reflection, fine scattered dots for sparkle, broad dilute arc for the shadow glaze
layers.highlights: The primary catchlight is the brightest point in the entire painting and should be crisp and confident. The secondary reflection is softer and more diffuse. The final blue glaze should be extremely dilute — just enough to unify and add depth without obscuring the layers beneath.

painting_plan.overall_notes: The painting builds from light to dark in the traditional watercolour manner, preserving luminosity in the early layers. The eye is centred on the canvas with the iris as the compositional focal point. Cool blues dominate throughout, unified by the pale background wash. Each layer allows previous washes to show through, creating the characteristic translucency of watercolour. The dreamy, ethereal quality is achieved through soft edges, wet-on-wet blooms, and the interplay of light and deep shadow. The final catchlight highlight is the compositional anchor that gives the eye its life and photorealistic quality.

strokes.batch_reasoning: The canvas is blank so the first priority is laying down the wet-on-wet background wash. Two large overlapping arcs with high thickness and low-to-mid opacity establish the pale ivory and cream base across the full canvas, mimicking a broad watercolour wash. A wide polyline across the upper-mid canvas introduces a faint cool blue horizontal band suggesting ambient light from the surroundings. Two splatter clusters in the upper-left and lower-right corners introduce the cool blue edge bleed characteristic of wet-on-wet watercolour blooms, while keeping the centre of the canvas lightest and most luminous as required by the layer plan.

This iteration completes the luminous background wash for Layer 1. Stroke 1 is a broad pale ivory arc sweeping across the canvas centre to reinforce the lightest, most luminous zone. Strokes 2 and 3 are cool blue arcs bleeding in from the upper-left and upper-right corners respectively, deepening the ambient cool-blue edge atmosphere established in iteration 1. Stroke 4 is a very soft, nearly transparent pale ivory filled circle centred on the canvas to concentrate the luminosity at the eye's future position. Stroke 5 is a cool blue splatter in the upper-right corner to add organic watercolour texture and reinforce the edge bleed. Together these strokes complete the wet-on-wet background wash with warm centre and cool periphery as described in the layer objectives.

This batch establishes the core eyelid skin structure for Layer 2. Stroke 1 is a broad, pale peachy-beige arc sweeping across the upper canvas to suggest the upper eyelid and brow area in the lightest base tone. Stroke 2 adds a slightly deeper peach arc over the same region to build warmth and suggest the shadow of the upper eyelid crease. Stroke 3 is a lower arc curving across the bottom half of the canvas to establish the under-eye and lower eyelid skin tone in a warm, light flesh colour. Stroke 4 is a polyline following the curve of the upper eyelid margin, adding a subtle rose-brown shadow hint at the eyelid fold for dimensional form. Stroke 5 is a smaller, deeper arc in the inner left corner to suggest the deeper shadow of the inner canthus area. All strokes use low opacity to maintain watercolour translucency and allow the luminous background to show through.

This batch finalises Layer 2. Stroke 1 is a broad warm rose-brown arc reinforcing the upper eyelid crease shadow with soft translucency. Stroke 2 adds a slightly tighter, darker rose-brown arc along the upper lid fold for dimensional depth. Stroke 3 is a gentle polyline following the upper lash line curve, softening the transition between eyelid and the eye opening to come. These five strokes build the sclera region in the almond-shaped eye opening. The first two broad arcs lay a pale blue-grey and cool off-white wash filling the lower and upper halves of the eye opening respectively, establishing the soft, slightly blue-tinted sclera tone. The third arc reinforces the left sclera (temporal side) with a slightly deeper cool wash. The fourth stroke is a delicate thin line in a faint rose-pink suggesting subtle veining near the inner corner, characteristic of a realistic sclera. The fifth arc lays a very dilute cool-blue shadow wash along the upper sclera edge, simulating the shadow cast by the upper eyelid — adding realism and depth while keeping the lower sclera brighter and more luminous. All strokes are kept very dilute and translucent to preserve the watercolour quality.

This batch establishes the iris with concentric filled circles creating a wet-on-wet layered effect: the outermost circle uses the deepest navy (#0D2240) to form the dark limbal ring, then progressively lighter prussian blue and cerulean washes build toward the centre, mimicking the natural tonal gradient of a real iris. The opacity is kept moderate to allow the sclera and skin layers beneath to show through, preserving watercolour translucency. The final arc stroke darkens the upper portion of the iris specifically, simulating the shadow cast by the upper eyelid — a key detail for photorealism. The concentric approach with varied opacity creates the bloom and merge effect characteristic of wet-on-wet watercolour technique.

This batch finalises Layer 4 objectives. Four radiating lines from the pupil centre (400, 370) outward in varied directions and blues simulate the fibrous texture of the iris — each at low opacity so they read as translucent watercolour washes rather than hard lines. The final stroke is a thick dark arc (0D2240, near-black navy) sweeping across the upper portion of the iris bounding box to reinforce the limbal ring darkness on the upper edge, which is shadowed by the eyelid. Together these strokes complete the iris fibre texture and limbal ring as specified in the layer description.

This batch addresses the core objectives of Layer 5. Stroke 1 paints the pupil as a deep near-black filled circle (~72px radius) centred at the iris centre (400, 370), crisp and dark as required. Strokes 2 and 3 establish the upper eyelid margin dark line — a broad dark arc sweeping across the upper lid edge, with a secondary arc for the inner lash line area, giving the eyelid margin definition. Strokes 4 and 5 add fine radiating iris detail lines in teal-blue from the pupil outward toward the upper-left and upper-right of the iris, beginning the fibre texture pass at varied opacity for natural watercolour variation.

This batch finalises Layer 5 by adding the remaining upper eyelash arcs fanning outward from the upper lid margin in very dark brown-black tones (characteristic of fine lash brushwork in watercolour), with varied arc bounding boxes to create the natural fan-spread of individual lashes. A secondary finer lash-line arc reinforces density near the centre of the upper lid. A sparse lower lash arc is added below the iris to suggest the shorter, sparser lower lashes. The strokes are kept thin (1-2px), dark, and slightly varied in opacity to give the organic, tapering quality of real lashes painted in watercolour. With these additions, the pupil, iris detail lines, upper lid margin, and both upper and lower lash arcs are all represented, meeting the Layer 5 objectives.

evaluations.feedback: The image shows a collection of soft, overlapping rectangular shapes rendered in very pale blue-grey and off-white tones against a white background. There is a translucent, layered quality to the shapes that does carry some resemblance to watercolour washes, particularly in the way lighter and slightly darker tones overlap. However, the shapes are too geometric and regular — they read more as digital design elements (cards or tiles) than as organic watercolour blooms. The wet-on-wet bleeding effect that is central to traditional watercolour background washes is largely absent; edges are too defined and the colour transitions lack the soft, unpredictable diffusion characteristic of pigment spreading on wet paper.

evaluations.feedback: This iteration shows a digitally rendered eye that bears only slight resemblance to traditional watercolour technique. evaluations.feedback: This image bears only slight resemblance to traditional watercolour painting.
